metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that F3's build-once/test-wheel/test-sdist/hash-gated-publish job graph is actually merged before this story is worked (or explicitly add those jobs to this story's scope), since they do not exist in the current release.yml.
evidence: Current release.yml has only: build → publish → github_release / mcp_registry; no test-wheel, test-sdist, or hash-gated jobs anywhere in .github/workflows/
Search for 'test-wheel', 'test-sdist', 'hash-gated', 'build-once' in .github/workflows/ returns zero matches
Plan states: 'the dispatch trigger must retain F3's build-once → test-wheel/test-sdist → hash-gated-publish job graph'
Ticket F3 is not resolvable (show_ticket('F3') returned 'Ticket F3 not found')
AC item 2 verifies the workflow 'still containing those jobs' — but those jobs don't exist yet
findings.finding: The plan treats F3's 'build-once → test-wheel/test-sdist → hash-gated-publish job graph' as a pre-landed baseline that this story composes with, but no such job graph exists in the current release.yml — only a bare `build` → `publish` chain with no test-wheel/test-sdist jobs or hash-gating — and F3 is not a resolvable ticket in the codebase.
findings.location: What section — 'Compose with — do not regress — F3 and F4'; AC item 2
scenarios: If F3 hasn't landed before this story, the AC ('verified by the workflow definition still containing those jobs') will trivially pass vacuously — the implementer re-keys guards from a job graph that was never added, or adds it themselves without F3's full scope review.
If F3 and F4 are meant to be co-implemented in this story, the scope section falsely excludes them as 'separate stories'.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit dependency gate in the AC: 'AC 0: release.yml already contains test-wheel, test-sdist, and hash-gated publish jobs (F3 landed) — confirmed by grep before starting implementation.' If F3 is not yet landed, block this story on F3 or fold the F3 deliverables into scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that F4's mcp_verify job and evidence-consolidation job are present in release.yml before this story begins (they currently are not), and block or expand scope accordingly.
evidence: Search for 'mcp_verify' in .github/workflows/ returns zero matches in release.yml
Search for 'evidence-consolidation' in .github/workflows/ returns zero matches
Current release.yml has a single mcp_registry job with no mcp_verify split
Plan states: 'retain F4's mcp_verify/mcp_registry split + evidence-consolidation job'
Ticket F4 is not resolvable (show_ticket('F4') returned 'Ticket F4 not found')
findings.finding: The plan treats F4's 'mcp_verify/mcp_registry split + evidence-consolidation job' as a pre-landed baseline, but neither `mcp_verify` nor any evidence-consolidation job exists in the current release.yml — only `mcp_registry` exists.
findings.location: What section — 'Compose with — do not regress — F3 and F4'; AC item 2
scenarios: Implementer re-keys dispatch guards on the existing single mcp_registry job, never creates the mcp_verify split, and passes AC item 2 because the AC's language ('still containing those jobs') doesn't catch the absence of jobs that were never added.

findings.finding: The env-preflight step — described as 'the single most security-critical gate' — is validated only through canned fixture JSON in unit tests (parser logic), and the operator-attested E2E staging run does NOT enumerate a scenario where this guard specifically fires (i.e., env-protection absent → release aborts). This is a proxy-validation gap: the live API call and the fail-closed abort decision are never exercised end-to-end in any testable artifact.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — unit test AC (AC line 5) and operator-attested E2E AC (AC line 6); 'What' section — 'Human authorization' bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC line 6 to include: 'a staging run where the pypi environment has required-reviewers cleared aborts at the preflight step (staging run URL attested).' This makes the fail-closed behavior of the live API call verifiable end-to-end, not merely as a parser fixture.

findings.finding: The preflight step's failure path — the release aborting when the `pypi` environment lacks required reviewers or has a non-`main`-only deployment-branch policy — is a new security-critical control flow that is never exercised in the stated testing plan. AC 5's three operator-attested staging scenarios cover non-`main` ref, version mismatch, and non-ancestor HEAD, but not the case where the preflight API call detects a misconfigured environment and fails closed. This is the highest-leverage guard in the plan ("the single most security-critical gate") yet its failure mode has no corresponding test attestation.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 4 (preflight step) and AC 5 (operator-attested guard-firing scenarios)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC 5 with: 'and a staging `pypi` environment temporarily missing required reviewers causes the preflight step to fail and abort the release before build' — exercised in the `navapbc/rebar-staging` fork against a TestPyPI environment configured without required reviewers, producing an attested failing run URL.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the expected GitHub Environments API response schema (specific field names and value vocabulary for `protection_rules`/`reviewers` and `deployment_branch_policy`) inline in the plan's preflight step description and in the unit-test fixture spec, so the parser and its negative-path test are unambiguously implementable.
evidence: Plan: 'a preflight step queries the GitHub REST API (`GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi`) and FAILS the release closed if the environment lacks required reviewers or its deployment-branch policy is not `main`-only'
Plan AC5: 'the env-preflight parser fails on env-protection-absent JSON'
ABSENCE: no plan section defines the GitHub API response field names or value vocabulary that the parser must inspect to determine 'required reviewers present' vs absent
ABSENCE: no fixture schema (even in outline) is given for the 'env-protection present/absent JSON' fixture that the unit test exercises
findings.finding: The env-preflight unit-test fixture is specified as 'env-protection present/absent JSON' but the plan never defines what JSON schema/field vocabulary constitutes 'protection present' vs 'protection absent' in the GitHub Environments API response. The decision rule ('lacks required reviewers or deployment-branch policy is not main-only') references two distinct API response properties whose key names (e.g., `protection_rules[].type`, `deployment_branch_policy.custom_branch_policies`, etc.) are never stated anywhere in the plan. An implementer writing the parser and its fixture is blocked — they must independently research the undocumented (from the plan's perspective) API response structure.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization — re-key the `pypi` environment + a fail-closed preflight'; AC line: 'the env-preflight parser fails on env-protection-absent JSON'
scenarios: Implementer writes a parser checking `response['protection_rules']` but the real API field is `response['reviewers']`; unit tests pass against the wrong fixture, live preflight silently passes even when protection is cleared
Two implementers independently write the parser using different field names from separate API research; the unit tests are internally consistent but test different (possibly wrong) schemas
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Human authorization' What section, add a concrete JSON excerpt (e.g., `{"protection_rules": [{"type": "required_reviewers", "reviewers": [...]}], "deployment_branch_policy": {"protected_branches": false, "custom_branch_policies": true}}`) showing both the passing and failing shapes; reference the GitHub REST API endpoint documentation by URL. Use these same excerpts as the unit-test fixture inputs.

findings.finding: The preflight step that calls `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi` does not specify its failure mode on transient API errors (timeout, 5xx, rate-limit, GitHub outage). The plan says the step 'FAILS the release closed' when protection is *absent*, but is silent on what happens when the API call itself fails — a fail-open implementation would silently skip the gate and permit a single-actor publish during a GitHub API outage.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization — re-key the `pypi` environment + a fail-closed preflight' and AC line: 'A release-time preflight step calls GET /repos/... and fails the release closed when required reviewers are absent'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the preflight AC: 'Any non-200 response from the GitHub API (including timeout and rate-limit) aborts the release with an explicit error; the step never skips the check on transport failure.' In the workflow step itself, use `|| { echo 'env-preflight API error — aborting'; exit 1; }` after the curl/gh call.

findings.finding: The plan's 'single most security-critical gate' — the pypi GitHub environment's required-reviewers setting — does not mechanically enforce 'a second human approves each publish' because GitHub deployment environment reviews, unlike PR reviews, permit the workflow dispatcher to self-approve their own review. If the person who dispatches the workflow is also listed as a required reviewer on the pypi environment, they can unilaterally approve and publish with no second human involved. The plan never specifies that the reviewer list must exclude the dispatcher, or that a team-reviewer pattern should be used so the dispatcher cannot be the sole approving member.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization' bullet; AC line 4 (preflight step AC)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a preflight check assertion: `required_reviewers >= 2` (so one self-approval is insufficient), OR specify that the pypi environment uses a team reviewer (e.g., a 'release-approvers' team) that excludes the dispatcher by org policy, and document this constraint in the AC and the preflight step specification.

findings.finding: The preflight step's fail-closed property is specified only for the policy-configuration-absent case, not for GitHub REST API errors (5xx, timeout, rate-limit). The plan says the step 'FAILS the release closed' if required_reviewers are absent or the branch policy is not main-only, but does not specify that a transient API error must also fail closed. An implementer following the plan literally could implement fail-open on API errors (e.g., treat a timeout as 'assume OK and proceed'), which would create a bypass: if the GitHub API is unreachable at release time, the preflight passes silently and a misconfigured environment could publish.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization' bullet; Scope section — preflight step specification; AC line 4
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the preflight step spec: 'treat any non-200 HTTP response from the environments API as a failure — exit non-zero, block the release. Only a 200 response with the correct policy shape should allow the release to proceed.'

findings.finding: The plan specifies that the preflight step 'fails the release closed' when the environment configuration is wrong, but does not specify behavior when the GitHub REST API call itself fails (network timeout, 5xx error, auth/rate-limit error). In a production security gate, an unhandled API-call failure that silently exits zero or is caught as 'no reviewers found' could open a publish path where protection was intended.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization' bullet; Scope section — '.github/workflows/release.yml preflight step'; AC line 4
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the preflight AC or the 'Human authorization' bullet: 'Any non-200 response, network failure, or schema parse error from the environments API also fails the release closed (the step uses `set -e` or equivalent and treats an absent/malformed response identically to a missing-reviewer response).'

findings.finding: The plan specifies a preflight step that calls `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi` and parses the response to detect 'required reviewers absent' and 'deployment-branch policy not main-only', but never names or verifies the GitHub REST API response schema fields it will parse — a non-trivial assertion given that the `deployment_branch_policy` object has two distinct modes (`protected_branches` vs. `custom_branch_policies`) with different JSON structures.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization — re-key the `pypi` environment + a fail-closed preflight'; AC line 4 ('A release-time preflight step calls `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi`')

findings.finding: The preflight step that calls `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi` does not declare the required `permissions:` grant for the workflow job, nor specify how non-200 responses (e.g., a 404 from insufficient token scope rather than a genuinely absent environment) are distinguished from a true 'environment not configured' response — leaving the fail-closed security guarantee on this boundary-crossing path with an undeclared and potentially ambiguous access contract.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization — re-key the pypi environment + a fail-closed preflight'; AC line: 'A release-time preflight step calls GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi and fails the release closed…'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the relevant AC or Scope bullet: 'The preflight job declares `permissions: contents: read` (or the minimum grant that allows the environments API call) and its error-handling treats any HTTP status other than 200 as an abort — a non-200 that could indicate a scope/permissions failure is logged with the raw status code and fails the step, ensuring a token-scope regression does not silently disable the reviewer gate.'

findings.finding: The preflight step asserts it can determine 'required reviewers absent' and 'deployment-branch policy is not main-only' by calling `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi`. This is an unverified capability assertion about the GitHub Environments REST API response shape. The GitHub API returns environment protection rules on this endpoint, but the specific field paths for required-reviewer presence and the deployment-branch-policy 'main-only' condition are not cited. A wrong field path would cause the preflight to silently pass (treating a missing field as 'protection present') — the opposite of the stated fail-closed intent.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization' bullet: 'a preflight step queries the GitHub REST API (`GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi`) and FAILS the release closed if the environment lacks required reviewers or its deployment-branch policy is not `main`-only'; AC4
findings.suggested_fix: In the preflight step design, reference https://docs.github.com/en/rest/deployments/environments#get-an-environment and specify: check `protection_rules` array for an entry with `type == 'required_reviewers'` and non-empty `reviewers`; check `deployment_branch_policy.protected_branches == true` (or the equivalent custom-branch-policy field). Add a test in the staging run (AC5) that deliberately clears the environment protection and confirms the preflight exits non-zero.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly enumerate `release.yml`'s `Wait for PyPI to serve this version` step (env `TAG: ${{ github.ref_name }}` → `version="${TAG#v}"`) and the `in_registry()` check in the `Publish server.json to the MCP Registry` step as required re-keying targets in the Scope or AC2, so the implementer substitutes `github.ref_name` with the `workflow_dispatch` version input in both shell-level derivations, not just in the `if:` guard.
evidence: release.yml:141: `TAG: ${{ github.ref_name }}`
release.yml:143: `version="${TAG#v}"`
release.yml:164 (in_registry): `v='${version}'` — version derived from the same TAG

Plan What section: 're-key every downstream version source and job guard from `github.ref_name` / the tag-context `if:` to the `workflow_dispatch` input' — identifies the need but not the specific wait-loop instance
Plan AC2: 'no `github.ref_name`/tag-`if:` version derivation remaining' — the wait-loop uses `github.ref_name` but via shell expansion, not a direct template expression, making it easy to miss
findings.finding: The mcp_registry job's wait-loop and in_registry() check both derive the release version via `version="${TAG#v}"` where `TAG: ${{ github.ref_name }}`. After the trigger changes to workflow_dispatch from main, `github.ref_name` evaluates to `main` (not a version tag), so `version="${TAG#v}"` silently produces `main` (the `${TAG#v}` bash expansion strips only a literal leading `v` prefix — absent on `main` — leaving `main` intact). This causes the PyPI wait step to poll `https://pypi.org/pypi/nava-rebar/main/json` for up to 5 minutes before failing with a misleading 'PyPI did not serve' error, and the `in_registry()` check queries for version `main` rather than aborting. The plan's AC2 says 'no `github.ref_name`/tag-`if:` version derivation remaining' but does not explicitly call out this specific wait-loop and registry-check as re-keying targets, creating a risk the implementer overlooks these two uses while updating the more obvious `if:` guard and `TAG` in the publish step.
findings.location: .github/workflows/release.yml lines 141-164 (mcp_registry job, 'Wait for PyPI to serve this version' step and 'Publish server.json to the MCP Registry' step); plan AC2 and Scope section
scenarios: Implementer re-keys the `if:` guard and the mcp-publisher publish step but misses the `TAG: ${{ github.ref_name }}` env var in the wait-loop step; the wait-loop polls for 5 minutes with 'nava-rebar/main' and fails, blocking MCP registry publication with a misleading error.
Implementer re-keys `if:` but leaves the `in_registry()` check using `version='main'`; the check never finds a match, causing unnecessary retry loops even if the registry already has the version.
findings.suggested_fix: In AC2, add: 'and the `mcp_registry` job's `Wait for PyPI to serve this version` step env var `TAG` and the `in_registry()` version derivation are also re-keyed from `github.ref_name` to the dispatch input.' In the Scope bullet for `release.yml`, add: 'including the mcp_registry job's shell-level `version="${TAG#v}"` derivations in both the wait-loop step and the retry-loop step.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the specific `TAG: ${{ github.ref_name }}` env-var references in `release.yml` lines 141 and 162 (the wait-loop and `in_registry()` steps) as explicit re-keying targets in the Scope or What section, so the implementer cannot miss these indirect uses of `github.ref_name`.
evidence: release.yml:141: `TAG: ${{ github.ref_name }}` in the 'Wait for PyPI to serve this version' step
release.yml:143: `version="${TAG#v}"` — version derived indirectly from `github.ref_name` via shell expansion
release.yml:162 (in_registry env block): `TAG: ${{ github.ref_name }}` again
Plan: 're-key every downstream version source and job guard from `github.ref_name` / the tag-context `if:` to the `workflow_dispatch` input' — general instruction, no enumeration of specific lines
ABSENCE: neither the Scope section nor the What section lists `release.yml:141` or `release.yml:162` as named re-keying targets
findings.finding: The plan's re-keying instruction ('re-key every downstream version source and job guard from `github.ref_name` / the tag-context `if:` to the `workflow_dispatch` input') is written as a general rule without enumerating the specific indirect uses in the existing `release.yml`. Concretely, the `mcp_registry` job contains TWO `TAG: ${{ github.ref_name }}` env-var references that feed bash expansion (`version="${TAG#v}"`) in the wait-loop step (line 141) and the `in_registry()` function (line 162) — these are not direct template expressions and are easily missed when following the instruction. The plan's own AC2 confirms the requirement ('no `github.ref_name`/tag-`if:` version derivation remaining') but does not name these specific locations as re-keying targets.
findings.location: What section — 'Compose with — do not regress — F3 and F4'; AC2: 'no `github.ref_name`/tag-`if:` version derivation remaining'; `.github/workflows/release.yml` lines 141 and 162
scenarios: Implementer re-keys the `if:` guards and the mcp-publisher `publish` step but misses the `TAG: ${{ github.ref_name }}` in the wait-loop; after the trigger change, `version` evaluates to `main`, causing the PyPI poll to request `/pypi/nava-rebar/main/json` and time out silently
Code review passes because the general instruction was followed and `github.ref_name` appears to be gone from the obvious locations
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section under `release.yml` changes, add: 'Re-key `TAG: ${{ github.ref_name }}` → `TAG: ${{ inputs.version }}` (or equivalent dispatch-input reference) in BOTH the "Wait for PyPI" step (line 141) and the "Publish server.json" step (line 162) of the `mcp_registry` job.'

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document (in `docs/releasing.md` and/or as inline workflow comments) the recovery procedure when post-publish steps fail after a successful PyPI upload, including how to re-run only the post-publish jobs or manually create the tag/GitHub Release — and consider making those post-publish steps idempotent (skip-if-tag-exists for tag creation, skip-if-release-exists for GitHub Release).
evidence: 'Once publish succeeds, the workflow creates the v* tag and the GitHub Release for the record/mirror'
No mention of idempotency, retry behavior, or operator guidance if post-publish steps fail after a successful PyPI upload
AC does not include a non-happy-path covering publish-succeeds-but-tag-fails
findings.finding: The plan specifies that the `v*` tag and GitHub Release are created AFTER a successful publish, but does not address the partial-failure recovery scenario where PyPI publish succeeds but the post-publish steps (tag creation, GitHub Release, MCP registry) fail. On a re-run attempt, PyPI will reject a duplicate upload of the same version, leaving the release in a broken half-completed state with no recovery path specified.
findings.location: What section — 'Tag + GitHub Release created AFTER a successful publish (record only)' and Scope bullet for `release.yml`
scenarios: PyPI upload succeeds; the subsequent `gh release create` step fails due to a transient API error. Re-running the workflow hits PyPI again, which rejects '409 File already exists' and aborts, leaving no GitHub Release or MCP registry entry.
MCP registry job fails after publish — operator has no documented recovery path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan (e.g. an expand-contract window where both tag and dispatch paths coexist before the tag path is removed, or a documented break-glass procedure for re-enabling the tag trigger) so the destructive removal of the tag-based release mechanism is reversible if the new dispatch mechanism fails.
evidence: 'No raw-tag publish path. With the environment keyed to dispatch-from-main, a hand-pushed v* tag cannot reach the pypi environment and therefore cannot publish — this eliminates the [arbitrary pushed tag publishes] surface'
'[operator-attested] The live pypi GitHub environment matches the codified terraform (required reviewers enabled, main-only)'
No plan text describes how to restore the tag-based flow, a parallel-operation window, or an emergency break-glass procedure if the dispatch mechanism is broken mid-release.
findings.finding: The plan explicitly removes the `push: tags: v*` publish trigger and re-keys the `pypi` environment from tag-based to dispatch-from-main, permanently closing the tag-based release path — yet provides no rollback or back-out plan if the new dispatch mechanism fails in production or if the environment re-key is done incorrectly.
findings.location: What section — 'No raw-tag publish path' bullet; Scope — '.github/workflows/release.yml' bullet; AC line: 'Remove the push: tags publish trigger'
scenarios: A maintainer needs to publish a critical security patch but the dispatch mechanism is broken (e.g. OIDC trust misconfigured, or the preflight step has a bug that always fails). With the tag path closed and no rollback documented, there is no safe recovery procedure.
The environment re-key is applied incorrectly (wrong branch policy) causing the preflight to permanently reject all releases; without a rollback path the release pipeline is stuck.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief 'Rollback' subsection to Scope: during the transition, keep `push: tags: v*` trigger active (but pointing at a no-op job or blocked by environment policy) for one release cycle as a canary; remove it only after one successful dispatch-from-main publish to production. Document a break-glass procedure: 'If the dispatch flow is broken, a repo admin may temporarily restore the tag trigger and revert the environment policy to unblock a critical release, then re-apply the dispatch constraints after the emergency.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify (in the preflight step design or `docs/releasing.md`) how the preflight distinguishes transient API failures (non-200, timeout) from a confirmed misconfiguration — e.g., surface the raw API HTTP status and response body in the workflow log, and optionally add a single retry with a short delay before failing.
evidence: 'a preflight step queries the GitHub REST API (GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi) and FAILS the release closed if the environment lacks required reviewers or its deployment-branch policy is not main-only'
ABSENCE: no mention of retry, timeout, or error-message differentiation for transient API failures vs. misconfiguration
findings.finding: The preflight `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi` call has no specified handling for transient GitHub REST API failures (timeouts, 5xx, rate-limits). The plan describes only the 'environment misconfigured' failure path; a transient API error would also fail the release closed with no distinguishing signal, leaving operators unable to determine whether the block was a real misconfiguration or an API hiccup.
findings.location: What section — 'a preflight step queries the GitHub REST API … and FAILS the release closed'; AC: 'A release-time preflight step calls GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi and fails the release closed'
scenarios: GitHub's API is briefly degraded (HTTP 503); the release fails with the same error message as a misconfigured environment, causing operators to investigate the environment settings rather than wait and retry.
findings.suggested_fix: In the preflight step script, emit the HTTP status code and response body to the workflow log on any non-200 response, and add one retry (e.g., `for i in 1 2; do … && break; sleep 10; done`) before failing, so transient 5xx errors do not permanently block a release without a clear signal.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a version-input normalization/validation step (strip optional leading 'v', assert semver format) before the lockstep comparison, and document the expected input format in the version field description in `release.yml` and `docs/releasing.md`.
evidence: 'A first job parses the intended version and every manifest and asserts exact equality before build: version_input == project.version == server.json.version == each server.json.packages[].version'
No mention of normalizing or validating the version_input format (with/without 'v' prefix, semver conformance) before comparison
The workflow_dispatch input is a free-text string supplied by the operator at dispatch time
findings.finding: The `workflow_dispatch` version input field has no specified validation for malformed or ambiguous input (e.g., 'v1.2.3' vs '1.2.3', non-semver strings, empty string) before the lockstep comparison runs. The lockstep step compares `version_input == project.version` but project.version presumably lacks the 'v' prefix, so a 'v'-prefixed input would silently fail with a confusing mismatch error rather than a clear 'invalid version format' message.
findings.location: What section — 'Version-lockstep, before build (pydantic pattern)' and AC line: 'version-lockstep step' in the first AC bullet
scenarios: Operator dispatches with 'v1.2.3' when pyproject.toml contains '1.2.3' — lockstep fails with a confusing mismatch rather than 'strip the v-prefix' guidance
Operator types '1.2' (non-semver) — the guard passes or fails inconsistently depending on manifest values
findings.suggested_fix: In the `workflow_dispatch` input definition, add a description: `'Version to release (without leading v, e.g. 1.2.3)'`, and as the first step of the lockstep job, strip any leading 'v' from the input and assert the result matches `^[0-9]+\.[0-9]+\.[0-9]+` before proceeding to the equality check.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `workflow_dispatch` version input's expected format and normalization contract (e.g., 'input must be bare semver without leading v; the lockstep step compares raw equality') in the What section and the `release.yml` input field's description, so AC5's unit-test fixture has an unambiguous normalization baseline.
evidence: Plan: 'A first job parses the intended version and every manifest and asserts exact equality before build: version_input == project.version == server.json.version == each server.json.packages[].version'
ABSENCE: no specification of whether `version_input` is normalized (v-prefix stripped, semver validated) before the equality check
Codebase: `pyproject.toml` stores versions as bare semver strings (e.g., '0.7.1') — confirmed by `tests/unit/test_version_sync.py` parsing `project.version`
Plan AC5: 'version-lockstep flags a deliberate mismatch' — the fixture for this test must know whether the input '1.2.3' equals or differs from 'v1.2.3'
findings.finding: The `workflow_dispatch` version input's expected format is never defined as a schema, yet the lockstep comparison protocol (`version_input == project.version == server.json.version == each server.json.packages[].version`) depends on it producing a normalized, comparable string. Since `pyproject.toml` stores versions without a leading 'v' prefix (confirmed: `pyproject.toml:10`) and operators may naturally type 'v1.2.3', the comparison rule is ambiguous: does the lockstep step strip a leading 'v' before comparing, or does it require exact equality? The unit test for 'version-lockstep flags a deliberate mismatch' cannot be written correctly without knowing the normalization contract.
findings.location: What section — 'Version-lockstep, before build (pydantic pattern)'; AC line: 'a version-lockstep step'; AC5: 'version-lockstep flags a deliberate mismatch'
scenarios: Operator dispatches with 'v1.2.3'; lockstep comparison fails because `pyproject.toml` has '1.2.3'; release is aborted with a confusing mismatch rather than a format error
Unit test author writes a fixture using 'v1.2.3' as `version_input` and '1.2.3' as `project.version`, expects a mismatch — but if the step normalizes, this fixture tests nothing useful
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `workflow_dispatch` input definition: `description: 'Version to release — bare semver, no leading v (e.g. 1.2.3). The lockstep step compares this directly against pyproject.toml project.version.'` and add a normalization step that strips a leading 'v' if present and rejects non-semver strings, so the comparison is always against a canonical form.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (or extend an existing one) that covers the release-evidence recording deliverable: specify what is recorded (approved SHA, version, manifest versions, artifact hashes), where it is stored (e.g. as a workflow summary, artifact, or step output), and how it is verified (e.g. codebase-verifiable from the workflow definition).
evidence: 'Record the approved SHA, version, manifest versions, and artifact hashes in release evidence.' (last bullet, What section)

Scanning all 9 ACs: AC1 covers trigger/guards, AC2 covers job-graph retention, AC3 covers OIDC publish and tag-after-publish, AC4 covers preflight, AC5 covers unit tests, AC6-AC7 are operator-attested, AC8 covers docs, AC9 covers lint/test. None mentions evidence recording or artifact hash logging.
findings.finding: The last bullet of the 'What' section describes a concrete deliverable — 'Record the approved SHA, version, manifest versions, and artifact hashes in release evidence' — but no acceptance criterion covers or verifies this deliverable, leaving it an orphaned described work item.

scenarios: An implementer reads the What section and builds the evidence-recording step, but it is never verified at review time because no AC names it — the feature may be silently dropped or implemented incompletely.
A reviewer cannot confirm the story is done because 'release evidence' has no verifiable criterion, creating ambiguity about whether the story is complete.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the CREATE owner and procedure for the `pypi` GitHub environment (e.g., an operator runbook step, a checked-in JSON/Terraform snippet even if not applied automatically), a named ownership responsibility, and a RETIRE/restore procedure — so the environment's required-reviewer + branch-policy state has a documented recovery path if cleared.
evidence: 'Rather than couple to the AWS-authed terraform-drift.yml … enforce it at release time: a preflight step queries the GitHub REST API … and FAILS the release closed if the environment lacks required reviewers'
'No infra/terraform-github/, terraform-drift.yml, or terraform-contract-test changes'
ABSENCE: no section defines who initially creates the environment, what the restore procedure is if it is deleted, or how ownership is enforced between releases
findings.finding: The `pypi` GitHub environment's required-reviewer and deployment-branch-policy configuration is unversioned shared state explicitly excluded from Terraform management, with no defined CREATE owner, no automated update/restore procedure, and no RETIRE/cleanup path — only operator attestation and a preflight drift-detection check. Any org admin can silently alter it between releases; the plan specifies no ownership, no CREATE procedure, and no way to restore it other than re-attesting.
findings.location: Scope section — 'No infra/terraform-github/ … changes'; AC: '[operator-attested] The live pypi GitHub environment matches the codified terraform'
scenarios: A new maintainer deletes or clears the `pypi` environment; the next release is blocked but there is no documented restore path — the team must reverse-engineer the correct settings.
Two org admins concurrently edit the environment settings; neither change is versioned, so the last write wins silently until the next release preflight fires.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a subsection to `docs/releasing.md` titled 'pypi environment lifecycle' that specifies: (a) who creates/owns it (e.g., the repo admin), (b) the exact required settings (required reviewers: [list], deployment branches: main only), (c) how to verify (link to the preflight check), and (d) how to restore if accidentally cleared. Even without Terraform automation this gives the lifecycle definition T9 requires.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief recovery procedure (in `docs/releasing.md` or as a workflow comment) for the partial-completion case where PyPI publish succeeds but tag/GitHub Release creation fails — e.g., confirm the PyPI release exists, then manually push the tag and create the GitHub Release via the API with the same version.

findings.finding: The plan does not specify a recovery procedure for partial completion when the PyPI OIDC publish succeeds but the subsequent `v*` tag creation or GitHub Release creation fails. A re-run would re-enter the version-lockstep and ancestry checks (which pass), attempt the publish again (PyPI rejects as duplicate upload), and then proceed to tag/release creation — but this re-run path is implicit, not stated.

The tag-creation step fails due to a permissions error (e.g., the GITHUB_TOKEN has no write access to refs); operators must manually create the tag and release without clear guidance.

After removing push:tags trigger, the rationale for 'no tag lock' changes: it is no longer 'because release.yml needs to push v* tags to publish' but rather 'because tags are now an output, not a trigger'
findings.finding: The plan marks `tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py` and its `test_module_is_main_only_no_tag_lock` test as unchanged, but the test's module docstring and an inline comment encode the rationale 'tags MUST stay human-pushable so release.yml can publish to PyPI on a v* tag push' / 'tags stay human-pushable (release.yml v* pushes) -> NO tag lock ruleset'. After this plan removes the `push: tags: v*` publish trigger, the test still passes (no tag lock remains correct), but the embedded rationale now points to an abolished publish path — creating a misleading comment that future readers (and the no-tag-lock invariant's justification) will encounter.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sentence in the 'Human authorization' bullet (or a threat-model note) acknowledging the TOCTOU window between the preflight passing and the deployment approval completing — clarifying that the preflight detects *pre-existing* missing protection but cannot prevent protection being stripped during an active run, and that this residual risk is accepted because the primary enforcement is GitHub's environment gate itself.
evidence: Plan: 'a preflight step queries the GitHub REST API... and FAILS the release closed if the environment lacks required reviewers or its deployment-branch policy is not `main`-only. This detects drift exactly when it matters (it blocks the actual publish, stronger than a periodic plan)'
Plan: 'anyone with `actions:write` can start a dispatch; only the environment's reviewers + branch policy enforce'
ABSENCE: the plan does not note that protection removed *after* preflight-pass but *before* the deployment approval prompt completes would bypass the second-human gate
findings.finding: The preflight step (querying `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi`) fires once at the start of the workflow run, before the environment's deployment-review approval prompt is presented. There is a TOCTOU window between a passing preflight and the moment a human approves the deployment: an org admin could silently remove required reviewers from the `pypi` environment during that window, and the deployment would then proceed without a second human's approval — exactly the single-actor publish the plan aims to prevent. The plan's own text acknowledges 'anyone with `actions:write` can start a dispatch; only the environment's reviewers + branch policy enforce' and that the preflight 'detects drift exactly when it matters (it blocks the actual publish)' — but this last claim is only true for pre-existing missing protection, not for protection removed after the preflight passes. The plan does not acknowledge this residual TOCTOU window or note it as an accepted risk.
findings.location: Plan 'What' section — 'Human authorization' bullet; AC4
scenarios: A malicious or compromised org admin starts a dispatch run, waits for the preflight to pass, then removes required reviewers from the `pypi` environment — the deployment prompt is skipped and the workflow proceeds to publish without any second-human approval.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (with a citation to GitHub API docs or an empirical test) that the default workflow `GITHUB_TOKEN` with `contents: read` returns the full `protection_rules` / `deployment_branch_policy` payload from `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi`; if elevated permissions or a PAT are required, specify them explicitly and update the plan's token-permission claim accordingly.
evidence: Plan: 'a preflight step queries the GitHub REST API (`GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi`) and FAILS the release closed if the environment lacks required reviewers or its deployment-branch policy is not `main`-only'
Plan: 'needs only the workflow's own read token'

GitHub REST API documentation for `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/{environment_name}` returns environment protection rules only for authenticated requests with sufficient repository permissions; free public tokens may return a stripped response

ABSENCE: the plan contains no citation to GitHub API documentation confirming that a default workflow token (no elevated permissions) can read environment protection rules, and no empirical test result is referenced
findings.finding: The plan asserts the environment-preflight step 'needs only the workflow's own read token' to call `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi` and reliably detect required-reviewer and deployment-branch-policy configuration — but this capability is unverified. GitHub's API for environment protection rules has historically required elevated permissions (repo admin or `repo`-scoped token) to return `protection_rules` / `deployment_branch_policy` detail; a default workflow `GITHUB_TOKEN` with `contents: read` may receive HTTP 200 with an empty or absent `protection_rules` array, causing the preflight parser to see no protection as 'protection present' or to silently pass, defeating the fail-closed guarantee.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization — re-key the `pypi` environment + a fail-closed preflight'; Scope section — 'a preflight step queries the GitHub REST API (`GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi`) … needs only the workflow's own read token'
scenarios: The workflow runs with the default `GITHUB_TOKEN` (`contents: read`); the API returns HTTP 200 with `protection_rules: []` or omits the field entirely; the preflight parser finds no reviewers absent and passes — a reviewer-less environment is not caught

An org admin clears required reviewers from the `pypi` environment; the preflight step silently passes because the token lacks permission to see the change; a single actor completes a publish without a second approval
findings.suggested_fix: Consult the GitHub REST API reference for `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/{environment_name}`. If the endpoint requires `repo` admin scope to return protection rules, add `environment: read` permission to the preflight job (available on GitHub Enterprise) or document that a PAT with `repo` scope is needed and store it as a secret — then update the plan's claim from 'needs only the workflow's own read token' to match. Alternatively, add an explicit assertion that the preflight step treats an *empty* `protection_rules` array as a failure (fail-closed on insufficient permissions), so degraded API responses don't silently pass.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) add a `github_repository_environment` Terraform resource (or equivalent IaC) for the `pypi` environment's required-reviewers and `main`-only deployment-branch policy so the most security-critical gate is reproducible as-code with a clear teardown, or (b) if the AWS-vs-GitHub auth split remains prohibitive, document in the plan the concrete manual restoration procedure (step-by-step console instructions + estimated SLA) for re-establishing correct environment protection after accidental deletion, and note this as a known residual risk accepted by the security owner.
evidence: 'No `infra/terraform-github/`, `terraform-drift.yml`, or terraform-contract-test changes' — the environment's protection rules are entirely outside version control
The AC marks environment correctness as [operator-attested] only, with no IaC artifact backing it
ABSENCE: no Terraform resource block, no `github_repository_environment` / `github_branch_protection` resource added for the `pypi` environment in the plan
findings.finding: The `pypi` GitHub environment's required-reviewers setting and deployment-branch policy are managed as an out-of-console operator-attested configuration, not as versioned IaC — the plan explicitly keeps `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` unchanged and defers environment setup to operator attestation. This means the environment is not reproducible as-code, has no defined teardown procedure, and offers no authoritative source of truth for auditors or a disaster-recovery scenario.
findings.location: Scope section — '`infra/terraform-github/main.tf` and its no-tag-lock contract test remain **unchanged**'; AC line '[operator-attested] The live `pypi` GitHub environment matches the codified terraform (required reviewers enabled, `main`-only) — attested by a `terraform plan` showing no drift or an org-admin confirmation.'
scenarios: An org-admin silently removes required reviewers from the `pypi` environment in the GitHub console between releases; the preflight catches it at the NEXT release attempt, but there is no IaC to restore the setting automatically or as a reviewed PR
A team member attempting to recreate the environment after an accidental deletion has no IaC to reference — only prose in `docs/releasing.md`
An auditor cannot verify the environment policy from the codebase alone; they must trust operator attestation
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `github_repository_environment` resource block to `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` (the GitHub Terraform provider already used in this file supports `deployment_branch_policy` and `reviewers`). If the AWS-auth split is truly blocking, at minimum add a `tests/unit/test_pypi_env_terraform.py` contract test that fails if the resource is absent, mirroring the existing `test_module_is_main_only_no_tag_lock` pattern — so the absence of IaC coverage is at least a codebase-visible, CI-enforced gap.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to `docs/releasing.md` (or its linked runbook) a preflight-failure remediation section specifying: who has authority to restore the `pypi` environment protection (role name or team), the exact GitHub console steps to re-establish required reviewers and the `main`-only branch policy, and any required re-review before the release can proceed.
evidence: 'a preflight step queries the GitHub REST API... and FAILS the release closed if the environment lacks required reviewers' — detection is specified
ABSENCE: no runbook entry, no escalation path, no restoration procedure is referenced for the post-detection state
'This detects drift exactly when it matters (it blocks the actual publish)' — but what happens next is unspecified
findings.finding: The preflight step that aborts a release when environment protection is missing has no specified remediation procedure or on-call path — the plan describes detection (fail the release) but not recovery (how to restore the environment, who is authorized, and within what SLA), leaving the drift-detected state operationally unresolved.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization — re-key the `pypi` environment + a fail-closed preflight'; Scope section — preflight step description
scenarios: The preflight fires during a time-sensitive security patch release; the team does not know who has org-admin rights to restore the environment or what exact settings to apply, adding hours to an urgent release

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify via GitHub API docs or an empirical test that `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/{env}` returns `protection_rules` and `deployment_branch_policy` fields with only the workflow's default `GITHUB_TOKEN` (no `administration: read`); if elevated permission is required, add it to the preflight job's `permissions:` block and document it in the plan.
evidence: Plan: 'a preflight step queries the GitHub REST API (`GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi`) and FAILS the release closed if the environment lacks required reviewers or its deployment-branch policy is not `main`-only'
Plan: 'needs only the workflow's own read token'

src/rebar/mirror_guard.py:164: 'Requires `administration:read` on `token` — in GitHub Actions grant the job `permissions: administration: read`; a fine-grained PAT is the documented fallback.' — same vendor, analogous API surface (repo rulesets vs. environment protection rules)
Partially-contradicted: the in-repo code for a similar GitHub API call explicitly requires elevated permissions, contradicting the plan's 'read token only' assertion for the environment endpoint
ABSENCE: plan cites no GitHub API documentation or empirical test confirming that a default GITHUB_TOKEN can read environment protection_rules fields
findings.finding: The plan asserts the environment-preflight step 'needs only the workflow's own read token' to call `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi` and reliably read required-reviewer and deployment-branch-policy fields — but this claim is contradicted by in-repo evidence. The existing mirror_guard.py (line 164) explicitly documents that GitHub's similar API (ruleset detail) 'Requires administration:read on token — in GitHub Actions grant the job permissions: administration: read.' GitHub's environment protection-rules endpoint has the same elevated-scope requirement; a default GITHUB_TOKEN with only contents: read (or no elevated scope) returns HTTP 200 with protection_rules: [] or omits the field, so the parser would see 'no reviewers' as indistinguishable from 'environment not protected' — the fail-closed guarantee silently fails open.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization — re-key the `pypi` environment + a fail-closed preflight'; Scope section: 'a preflight step queries the GitHub REST API (`GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi`) … needs only the workflow's own read token'
scenarios: Workflow runs with default GITHUB_TOKEN (contents: read); GET /environments/pypi returns HTTP 200 with protection_rules: [] or missing; preflight parser sees empty array and incorrectly concludes 'protection is absent' — but its logic is written to fail only if 'required_reviewers count == 0', so an empty array matches and correctly fails. HOWEVER: the alternative scenario is the parser is written to succeed only if 'required_reviewers > 0 found', in which case an empty array also correctly fails. The real risk is a 404 or 403 being silently swallowed, or the field being absent from the response entirely causing a KeyError that the parser must handle without swallowing.

More critically: if the endpoint requires `environments:read` or admin scope and returns 404 to a default token on a private repo (or even a public repo with environment protection), the preflight step would need to treat a 404 as 'protection absent' (fail-closed), but the plan does not specify this error handling.

An operator grants `permissions: environment: read` (if that scope exists) or `administration: read` — but the plan says no elevated permission is needed, so implementers may not add it, leaving the step broken on first run.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `permissions: administration: read` (or the correct GitHub Actions scope) to the preflight job, mirroring what mirror_guard.py already documents for the analogous ruleset-detail call. Alternatively, test the endpoint with a default token first and add error handling that treats a 403/404 as 'protection absent' (fail-closed) rather than as a pass.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Require the plan to specify that the `mcp_registry` job pins the `mcp-publisher` download to an exact version URL and verifies its SHA-256 checksum before execution, eliminating the unpinned `releases/latest` fetch on a job holding `id-token: write`.
evidence: Plan: 'retain F4's mcp_verify/mcp_registry split + evidence-consolidation job — the trigger change swaps only how the run starts and is authorized, not the internal job structure'

release.yml:129: `curl -L "https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/registry/releases/latest/download/mcp-publisher_..." | tar xz mcp-publisher` — 'latest' is unpinned, no --sha256 / hash verification before execution
release.yml:118: `id-token: write` is on the mcp_registry job that runs the downloaded binary
ABSENCE: the plan contains no requirement to pin the mcp-publisher version or verify its checksum before execution on the release path
findings.finding: The `mcp_registry` job retains an unpinned, unverified external binary fetch (`curl -L .../releases/latest/download/mcp-publisher...` — no version pin, no SHA-256 check) executed in the same job holding `id-token: write`. The plan's 'Compose with — do not regress — F3 and F4' clause carries this pattern forward without requiring pinning or integrity verification. A compromised `modelcontextprotocol/registry` release feed or a MITM on the download URL would inject arbitrary code into a CI runner that holds OIDC tokens capable of publishing to PyPI and the MCP Registry — a full privilege-escalation on the boundary-crossing path.
findings.location: What section — 'Compose with — do not regress — F3 and F4 (this story lands last)'; current `release.yml:127-129` (mcp_registry job, Install mcp-publisher step)
scenarios: A compromised GitHub release on modelcontextprotocol/registry replaces the mcp-publisher binary; the next release run fetches and executes the malicious binary under id-token:write, which exfiltrates the OIDC token and uses it to publish a malicious package to PyPI.
A network-level MITM (e.g. a rogue DNS or CDN edge) serves a tampered archive to the GitHub Actions runner; no checksum verification prevents execution.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section and the relevant AC, add: 'The `mcp_registry` job must pin `mcp-publisher` to a specific release tag (not `latest`) and verify the downloaded archive against a checked-in SHA-256 before executing it (e.g., `echo "<expected-sha256>  mcp-publisher.tar.gz" | sha256sum -c`).' The pinned version and hash should be stored in the workflow or a companion file and updated via a deliberate runbook step when upgrading.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the required GitHub API permission level for the preflight's `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/{name}` call (citing the API docs or an empirical test), specify the exact token scope needed, and add a unit-test fixture for the case where the API returns HTTP 200 with an empty or absent `protection_rules` array (treating it as fail-closed, not as 'protection present').
evidence: Plan: 'a preflight step queries the GitHub REST API (`GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi`) and FAILS the release closed if the environment lacks required reviewers'
Plan: 'needs only the workflow's own read token'

ABSENCE: the plan contains no citation to GitHub API documentation confirming the default workflow GITHUB_TOKEN (contents: read) returns the full protection_rules / deployment_branch_policy fields, and no empirical test result or unit-test fixture for the 'API returns empty protection_rules' case is referenced
findings.finding: The plan's preflight step calls `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi` with 'only the workflow's own read token' and asserts it will reliably detect missing required reviewers and a non-`main`-only deployment-branch policy. The access posture of this boundary-crossing path is undeclared: the plan does not state what GitHub API permission level is required to receive `protection_rules` and `deployment_branch_policy` detail in the response, nor does it cite API documentation or a tested result. If the default workflow token returns HTTP 200 with an empty or absent `protection_rules` array, the fail-closed preflight silently passes — inverting the security guarantee it is meant to provide.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization — re-key the `pypi` environment + a fail-closed preflight'; Scope section — 'a preflight step queries the GitHub REST API … needs only the workflow's own read token'
scenarios: The workflow's GITHUB_TOKEN lacks the permission level required for the environments API to return protection rules; the API returns HTTP 200 with `protection_rules: []`; the preflight parser interprets this as 'no protection' — or worse, the parser only checks for HTTP 200 and treats the response as valid, silently passing a cleared environment.

findings.finding: The described work in Scope explicitly states this story 'rewrites the now-obsolete existing sections it invalidates — the "Tag → PyPI publishes automatically" procedure and the "Hard rules" manual-lockstep framing', but AC #7 only verifies that `docs/releasing.md` *adds* new content ('documents the dispatch-from-main process, the version-lockstep invariant, the environment re-key, and the reviewer requirement'). It does not verify removal of the old tag-based procedure and manual-lockstep sections. A partial doc update that retains the contradictory old procedure would pass AC #7 as written.
findings.location: Scope — `docs/releasing.md` entry; AC #7
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite AC #7 as: '`docs/releasing.md` removes (or clearly marks as superseded) the "Tag → PyPI publishes automatically" procedure and the "Hard rules" manual-lockstep section, AND documents the dispatch-from-main process, the version-lockstep invariant, the environment re-key, and the reviewer requirement.'

findings.finding: The preflight step's GitHub API permission requirement is asserted but not specified: the plan states the step 'needs only the workflow's own read token' to call GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi and inspect protection_rules (required reviewers + deployment-branch policy), but the exact GITHUB_TOKEN permission scope needed for that endpoint is unstated. If the workflow's token lacks the necessary permission (e.g. environments: read is required in fine-grained tokens, or admin access is needed for protection_rules on private repos), the preflight silently returns an empty or truncated response and the fail-closed guarantee is lost.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization — re-key the pypi environment + a fail-closed preflight' bullet; Scope section first bullet; AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Add `environments: read` to the job's `permissions:` block for the preflight step, and note in the plan that this permission is required for the `protection_rules` field to be populated in the API response. Verify with: `curl -H 'Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN' https://api.github.com/repos/{owner}/{repo}/environments/pypi | jq '.protection_rules'` in a test run.

findings.finding: The env-preflight step's behavior under GitHub API errors (HTTP 4xx, 5xx, timeout, or malformed/empty response) is not addressed by any test or AC. If the API call fails for reasons unrelated to missing protection (e.g., transient 500, token scope issue), the step could fail-open (allowing publish) or produce a confusing hard-fail that blocks legitimate releases — neither outcome is specified or tested.
findings.location: 'What' section — 'Human authorization / preflight step' bullet; Acceptance Criteria — AC line 4
findings.suggested_fix: Add two additional unit-test fixtures to the env-preflight test: one where the API returns a non-200 status (assert the step fails closed) and one where the response body is malformed JSON (assert fail closed). Document the intended behavior in the preflight step's inline comment or the releasing.md update.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the implementation form for the extracted guard helpers (e.g., Python functions in `src/` or `scripts/` called from the YAML step, with the ancestry check using a `pytest` fixture that initializes a real temp git repo via `git init` + test commits), so AC5's proving path via `make test` is unambiguous.
evidence: 'Extract the three guards into small testable units — the version-lockstep comparison, the ancestry check, and the env-preflight response parsing — and unit-test them with fixtures'
AC5: 'the ancestry check rejects a non-ancestor SHA (and accepts a non-tip ancestor)' — requires a git repo or a mock, neither specified
Plan says `make test` is the proving command but does not specify how git-invoking code becomes pytest-reachable
findings.finding: The plan instructs an executor to 'extract the three guards into small testable units' and unit-test them via `make test`, but does not specify the implementation form for the guards — particularly the ancestry check (`git merge-base --is-ancestor`). An executor cannot determine without clarification whether to (a) write Python wrapper functions calling subprocess, (b) create standalone shell scripts tested via pytest-subprocess or similar, or (c) use a real in-repo git fixture. Choosing the wrong form (e.g., inline bash left in the YAML) would make AC5 unreachable.
findings.location: What section ('Verify the guard LOGIC with unit tests'); AC5
scenarios: Executor implements the ancestry check as inline bash in the YAML and cannot write a pytest fixture for it, leaving AC5 partially unverifiable
Executor writes a Python subprocess wrapper but has no guidance on whether to use a real git fixture (fragile) or a mock (requires a mock library decision)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-sentence decision to the plan: 'Implement the three guards as Python functions (e.g., in `scripts/release_guards.py`) invoked from the YAML `run:` steps via `python scripts/release_guards.py <subcommand>`; the ancestry check uses a real temporary git repo created in the pytest fixture (no subprocess mock needed).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or `CLAUDE.md` / design-doc section capturing the architectural decision to use a fail-closed release-time preflight (GitHub REST API gate) instead of IaC enforcement, including the explicit tradeoff rationale (AWS-vs-GitHub auth split, self-contained control, detects drift at publish time).
evidence: 'Rather than couple to the AWS-authed `terraform-drift.yml` (a different provider/auth model), enforce it at release time: a preflight step queries the GitHub REST API'
'No `infra/terraform-github/`, `terraform-drift.yml`, or terraform-contract-test changes'

The `docs/releasing.md` scope is limited to: 'document the dispatch-from-main process, the version-lockstep invariant, the environment re-key, and the reviewer requirement' — no mention of capturing the architectural tradeoff decision
T5e requires 'A novel architectural decision is captured in an ADR / CLAUDE.md / design-doc update'; T7 requires 'A new pattern/contract/config/CLI gets a doc/ADR'
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision — a fail-closed release-time preflight that gates publication on a live GitHub REST API call instead of IaC enforcement — but scopes only an operational `docs/releasing.md` update, not an ADR or CLAUDE.md entry capturing the architectural rationale and the explicit tradeoff against terraform-based enforcement.
findings.location: Scope section (`docs/releasing.md` bullet) and 'Human authorization' bullet in What section
scenarios: A future maintainer inherits the release pipeline, encounters the preflight step, and has no architectural record of why it exists instead of terraform enforcement — they may remove or bypass it thinking it is redundant with IaC
A security reviewer auditing the release controls cannot find the rationale for the preflight-over-IaC choice and may incorrectly assess it as an oversight
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/NNN-release-preflight-over-iac.md` (or add a 'Architecture decisions' subsection to `docs/releasing.md`) that records: (1) context — the terraform auth split makes drift-workflow coupling disproportionate; (2) decision — fail-closed preflight at release time; (3) consequences — the step must be kept in `release.yml` and its logic kept in sync with the environment's expected configuration.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the ancestry assertion unit test (AC5, 'rejects a non-ancestor SHA') is necessary given the plan's own acknowledgement that dispatch-from-main + the ref guard make the non-ancestor path unreachable in production; consider replacing the extracted-helper + fixture with an inline workflow shell assertion and documenting it as non-testable-without-live-git rather than adding a standalone test module for a path that cannot fire.
evidence: Plan quote: 'With dispatch-from-main this is redundant with the ref guard, kept as defense-in-depth.'
AC5 mandates a full unit test for this guard: 'the ancestry check rejects a non-ancestor SHA (and accepts a non-tip ancestor)'
Existing merge-base usage in tests/unit/test_sync_union_recovery.py:74,77 shows inline git calls suffice for git-ancestry assertions without a dedicated extracted helper
The ref guard ('enforce github.ref == refs/heads/main') already prevents any non-main-ancestor SHA from entering the workflow under dispatch
findings.finding: The plan explicitly labels the ancestry assertion ('git merge-base --is-ancestor') as redundant with the ref guard ('With dispatch-from-main this is redundant with the ref guard, kept as defense-in-depth'), yet AC5 mandates a dedicated unit test for it ('the ancestry check rejects a non-ancestor SHA (and accepts a non-tip ancestor)'). Under the new dispatch model, a non-ancestor SHA cannot reach this step in production — the ref guard blocks it first. Building a test harness for a control path that cannot be the deciding gate in production is YAGNI scope: it adds complexity (a git-repo fixture, a bogus-SHA fixture) to test a branch that cannot fire under the designed flow, without adding security coverage beyond what the ref guard already provides.
findings.location: What section — 'Ancestry assertion (belt-and-suspenders)' bullet; AC line 5 ('the ancestry check rejects a non-ancestor SHA')
scenarios: The ancestry unit-test infra (git-repo fixture, bogus-SHA path) adds implementation effort and maintenance surface to prove a code-path that the primary control (dispatch + ref guard) already makes unreachable in practice.
If the ancestry step is kept as a defensive shell one-liner rather than an extracted Python helper, no unit test is needed and the same defense-in-depth value is preserved.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
